questions.question: What axiom distinguishes a partial order from an equivalence relation?
options: Reflexivity
Transitivity
Antisymmetry — partial orders require `a ≤ b ∧ b ≤ a ⇒ a = b`, while equivalences require symmetry (`a ~ b ⇒ b ~ a`)
Totality
questions.explanation: Both are reflexive and transitive. The third axiom flips: equivalences are symmetric (`a~b ⇒ b~a`), partial orders are antisymmetric (`a≤b ∧ b≤a ⇒ a=b`). Symmetry says related-in-both-directions is always OK; antisymmetry says it forces equality.

questions.question: On a Hasse diagram of the subset poset 𝒫({a,b,c}), which edges are drawn?
options: All `A ⊆ B` pairs
Only the *cover* pairs: each A is connected to B iff A ⊂ B and no strict intermediate set exists
Only the reflexive ones
Only pairs where B \ A is a singleton
questions.explanation: Hasse diagrams omit implied edges (transitivity) and self-loops (reflexivity). For subsets, the cover pairs are exactly those where B has exactly one more element than A — which happens to coincide with option D for THIS poset, but generally the definition is 'no strict intermediate.' Both descriptions yield the same picture for 𝒫({a,b,c}).

questions.question: What does `join(x, y)` in a poset mean?
options: x ∪ y
The least upper bound of {x, y} — the smallest z with both x ≤ z and y ≤ z
x · y
The maximum of x and y
questions.explanation: Join is the LUB: smallest element above both. For subsets, that's ∪. For divisibility, that's lcm. For an arbitrary poset, the join may not exist (no UB at all, or multiple incomparable least UBs). A *lattice* is a poset where joins and meets always exist for pairs.

questions.question: Kahn's topological sort produces what, and how fast?
options: A total ordering of any set, in O(n log n)
A linear extension of a DAG (one of possibly many) in O(V + E); fails iff a cycle exists
A spanning tree in O(V + E)
A topological invariant of the graph
questions.explanation: Kahn's algorithm produces a valid linear extension (a total order extending the partial order) of any DAG in O(V + E). If it can't drain all nodes, there's a cycle — that's the standard cycle-detection algorithm for directed graphs. Make/Bazel/Cargo run a version of this on every build.

questions.question: ℕ under divisibility (`a | b ⇔ a divides b`): is it a lattice?
options: No — not even a partial order
Yes — join = lcm, meet = gcd
Yes — but only on prime numbers
Only finite subsets are lattices
questions.explanation: ℕ with divisibility is a classic lattice example. Join is lcm (least common multiple — smallest above both), meet is gcd (greatest common divisor — largest below both). 1 is the bottom (divides everything). It's an unbounded lattice — no top — but every pair has a join.

questions.question: Why do dataflow analyses in compilers (constant propagation, available expressions) use lattices?
options: Tradition
The analysis iterates by taking joins of incoming abstract values at merge points; the lattice structure guarantees the iteration reaches a fixed point (and bounds its size)
Lattices are fast to evaluate
They aren't related to lattices
questions.explanation: Dataflow analyses are fixed-point computations over abstract value lattices. At every CFG merge point, take the join of incoming abstract values; iterate until nothing changes. The lattice's *height* (longest ascending chain) bounds the number of iterations. This framework (Cousot's abstract interpretation) is the foundation of static analysis.
